1960s.title: Roads, trade, and a narrow price base
1960s.body: Nepal's early CPI record sits in a smaller, more trade-dependent economy where food supply, transport links, and Indian price movements mattered heavily.
1960s.event: Early development planning and transport expansion
1960s.driver: Food supply, transport costs, Indian price spillovers
1960s.outcome: Small changes in supply chains could move household prices strongly.
1960s.evidence: World Bank / IMF IFS annual CPI

1970s.title: Oil shock reaches a mountain economy
1970s.body: Global oil shocks raised transport and import costs, which mattered in a landlocked economy dependent on cross-border trade routes.
1970s.event: Global oil shock and import-cost pressure
1970s.outcome: Purchasing power was shaped by prices moving through trade corridors.

1980s.title: Managed economy under trade constraints
1980s.body: State controls, import dependence, and limited industrial depth kept prices sensitive to food supply and cross-border conditions.
1980s.event: Pre-1990 political economy and trade dependence
1980s.outcome: Inflation was not just monetary; it was also logistical.
1980s.evidence: World Bank / IMF IFS annual CPI

1990s.title: Democracy, liberalization, and open borders
1990s.body: After the 1990 democratic transition, liberalization and labor migration changed incomes, while the India link remained central to price dynamics.
1990s.outcome: Household purchasing power increasingly reflected remittance income and imported-price pressure.

2000s.title: Conflict, remittances, and food prices
2000s.body: The civil conflict, migration, and the global food and fuel shock all affected household budgets through employment, remittances, and staple prices.
2000s.event: Civil conflict period and 2006 peace transition
2000s.outcome: The price index reflects both domestic instability and global commodity pressure.
2000s.evidence: World Bank / IMF IFS annual CPI

2010s.title: Earthquake and border-disruption price shock
2010s.body: The 2015 earthquake and subsequent border disruption made reconstruction materials, fuel, and everyday goods more expensive for many households.
2010s.outcome: Physical disruption translated directly into purchasing-power stress.

2020s.title: Pandemic recovery and imported inflation
2020s.body: COVID-19, remittance swings, fuel prices, and imported inflation shaped the early 2020s purchasing-power experience.
2020s.event: Pandemic reopening and global commodity pressure
2020s.outcome: The 2024 endpoint shows a much higher price level than the 1960s base years.
